title: Affirmations and Denials Regarding Abolitionist Terms and Other Controversial Subjects
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman responds to recurring criticisms of Free the States by issuing a doctrinal-style series of affirmations and denials covering abolitionist terminology, idolatry accusations, the salvation of incrementalists, and motives behind drawing sharp lines with the Pro-Life Movement. The piece is meant as Free the States' definitive on-the-record answer so the staff can move on from these debates.
audiences: Pro-life Christian considering whether to adopt the abolitionist label
Abolitionist activist accused of being divisive or tribal
Pastor or church leader weighing whether abolitionism is idolatrous
Movement critic alleging youthful vanity or credit-seeking
New abolitionist trying to understand partnership boundaries

questions.question: Why does Free the States insist on distinguishing the Abolitionist Movement from the Pro-Life Movement instead of just sharing the 'pro-life' label?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian considering whether to adopt the abolitionist label
questions.answer: Because the two movements hold meaningfully different ideological tenets, and blurring the labels obscures the choice people need to make between them. Without a clear category distinction, abolitionist ideas, bills, and candidates cannot be communicated or contrasted with their pro-life counterparts, and the movement's growth stalls.
questions.quote: We Affirm that distinguishing the Abolitionist Movement from the Pro-Life Movement is crucial to clearly communicating the ideological distinctions between the tenets of abolitionism and the tenets of pro-lifeism; and that the maintenance of this ideological distinction is crucial toward people understanding the difference between abolitionism and pro-lifeism, abolition bills and pro-life bills, abolitionist candidates and pro-life candidates.

questions.question: Is fighting over the term 'pro-life' a productive use of abolitionist energy?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist accused of being divisive or tribal
questions.answer: No. Silberman explicitly says that battling over ownership of 'pro-life' produces confusion rather than clarity and actively stunts the movement's growth. The point is to maintain a clean ideological distinction, not to litigate vocabulary.
questions.quote: Fighting over who gets to use the term “pro-life” will lead to confusion and will stunt the growth of the movement.

questions.question: Are people who hold abolitionist convictions but still call themselves 'pro-life' in sin for that choice?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian considering whether to adopt the abolitionist label
questions.answer: No. Free the States considers it an unwise decision but explicitly denies it is a sin. The label issue is a strategic and clarity question, not a charge of moral guilt against the individual.
questions.quote: We Deny that those who affirm the tenets of abolitionism but choose to use a different label, including “pro-life,” are in sin for that unwise decision.

questions.question: Is Free the States being petty or tribal by drawing such sharp lines between abolitionism and pro-lifeism?
questions.audience: Movement critic alleging youthful vanity or credit-seeking
questions.answer: Silberman flips the accusation: he says it is often pride, tribalism, and pettiness that drive people who already believe abolitionist tenets to keep clinging to the 'pro-life' identity. Pro-life pride, in his view, is the deeper motive at play, not abolitionist tribalism.
questions.quote: On the contrary, it is often for petty, tribal, and/or prideful reasons that people who have come to embrace the tenets of abolitionism insist on still calling themselves “pro-life.” Pro-life pride in particular plays a big role here.

questions.question: Does identifying as 'pro-life' automatically disqualify a person or organization from partnership with abolitionists?
questions.audience: New abolitionist trying to understand partnership boundaries
questions.answer: No. Free the States is willing to work with people and organizations who use the pro-life label. The concern is that some who claim abolitionist ideology while clinging to the pro-life label are actually resisting parts of abolitionism, and those underlying ideological gaps may or may not preclude partnership case-by-case.
questions.quote: We Deny that an individual or organization choosing to identify as “pro-life” is enough reason to not partner with them. We are willing to work with such people and organizations.

questions.question: How does Free the States justify treating abortion as a uniquely high priority compared to other kingdom causes?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader weighing whether abolitionism is idolatrous
questions.answer: By echoing R.C. Sproul: a church silent during a holocaust ceases to be a real church. Other kingdom work is real and important, but the active mass killing of image-bearers takes priority over nearly all of it, and the church must act as if preborn neighbors actually are image bearers.
questions.quote: We Affirm, with R.C. Sproul, that “When the church is silent in the midst of a holocaust, she ceases to be a real church.” Busyness with other things, even important kingdom endeavors, is no excuse for being apathetic regarding a present holocaust.

questions.question: Is idolatry of abolitionism a real, widespread problem inside the movement?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader weighing whether abolitionism is idolatrous
questions.answer: Silberman denies it is widespread. Because abolitionism is defined as the gospel applied to the evil of the age, anyone who elevates the movement above the gospel ceases to be a real abolitionist by definition. Gospel-centeredness is a non-negotiable tenet, which makes the idolatry charge particularly off-target.
questions.quote: We Deny that idolatry of abolitionism is a widespread problem in the Abolitionist Movement. Abolitionism is simply the gospel applied to the evil of the age. The gospel and obedience to Christ is our highest objective. An abolitionist who elevates anything above the gospel of Jesus Christ ceases to be a real abolitionist.

questions.question: How does Free the States contrast the Abolitionist Movement with most political movements, including pro-life organizations?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader weighing whether abolitionism is idolatrous
questions.answer: Most political movements, including the major pro-life organizations, are built on ecumenical big-tent strategy and operate as officially secular groups so as not to be dismissed as religious. The Abolitionist Movement, by contrast, refuses to subordinate gospel proclamation to political pragmatism, which is rare in the political world.
questions.quote: Virtually every political movement in existence is rooted in ecumenicism and big tent philosophies which are viewed by worldly thinkers as the only way to social and political change. Nearly all of the major pro-life organizations are officially secular organizations, and they refuse to integrate the gospel into their mission for fear of being labeled a religious movement by abortion supporters.

questions.question: What is Free the States' position on incrementalist anti-abortion strategy?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christian considering whether to adopt the abolitionist label
questions.answer: They affirm that incrementalism, defined as changing abortion law by degrees, is unbiblical and that it actively delays abolition. It is not viewed as a slower path to the same goal but as an obstacle to that goal.
questions.quote: We Affirm that an incrementalist approach toward fighting the abortion holocaust (ie. a strategy of changing abortion law by degrees) is unbiblical and that it delays the abolition of abortion.

questions.question: Who does Free the States blame for the continued legality of abortion in heavily Republican states?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist accused of being divisive or tribal
questions.answer: Pro-life incrementalist politicians, lobbyists, organizations, thought leaders, and the pro-lifers who support them. In states like Oklahoma, Texas, Idaho, Indiana, Arizona, South Carolina, and Missouri, where pro-life Republicans hold all the levers, those incrementalists are named as the primary obstacle to abolition.
questions.quote: We Affirm that pro-life incrementalist politicians, lobbyists, organizations, thought leaders, and the pro-lifers who support them are the primary obstacles to the abolition of abortion in states like Oklahoma, Texas, Idaho, Indiana, Arizona, South Carolina, Missouri, and others where pro-life Republicans control all the levers of power in the State government.

questions.question: Does Free the States believe pro-life incrementalists can be saved Christians?
questions.audience: Pastor or church leader weighing whether abolitionism is idolatrous
questions.answer: Yes. Silberman explicitly denies the idea that pro-life incrementalists cannot be saved and grants that there are many well-meaning, born-again believers within the Pro-Life Movement. He treats it as somewhat absurd that this even has to be clarified.
questions.quote: We Deny the idea that pro-life incrementalists cannot be saved. On the contrary, there are many well-meaning, born again believers in the Pro-Life Movement. It is crazy that we have to clarify of our view on this, but unfortunately, we do.

questions.question: Why does Free the States care so much about the historical narrative of how abortion gets abolished?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist accused of being divisive or tribal
questions.answer: Because they want future generations to credit the church awakening from apathy and embracing biblical abolitionist tenets, not worldly pragmatism. That historical record matters as a testimony for future believers fighting their own age's evils.
questions.quote: We Affirm that it is our desire that when abortion is abolished, commentators and historians and everyone else knows that it was abolished because the Church of Jesus Christ awoke from its apathy and rejected worldly pragmatism by embracing the tenets of abolitionism. That this occurs will be an important testimony for future generations to look at and learn from as they fight the evil of their age.

questions.question: Is Free the States drawing distinctions with the Pro-Life Movement out of youthful vanity or platform-building?
questions.audience: Movement critic alleging youthful vanity or credit-seeking
questions.answer: Silberman argues the financial incentive runs the opposite direction. The Pro-Life Movement holds the money, political access, and large platforms, so anyone seeking those things would gravitate to it. Choosing the Abolitionist Movement is choosing harder truth-telling over access, even if that gap is shrinking as abolitionism grows.
questions.quote: On the contrary, the Pro-Life Movement is where the money, political connections, and huge platforms are. Those seeking large platforms and influential partnerships will be drawn to identify with the Pro-Life Movement. Those willing to speak the hard truth without regard for access to larger platforms and bigger tents will be drawn to the Abolitionist Movement.

questions.question: How does Silberman defend Russell Hunter against the charge of seeking personal credit?
questions.audience: Movement critic alleging youthful vanity or credit-seeking
questions.answer: By personal testimony. He calls Hunter the least credit-seeking leader he has ever worked with: Hunter routinely platforms others instead of himself, dislikes putting his name on things, and explicitly tells staff to disagree with him in meetings. Unlike most organizations that exist to grow the CEO's brand, Hunter is described as zealous for the tenets of abolitionism but indifferent to his own reputation.
questions.quote: Russell, who started the Abolitionist Movement and founded Free the States, takes every opportunity to platform others instead of himself. While I would naturally give him radio and podcast appearances that we are offered, he often tells me that people need to know me better and that I should do them. He doesn’t like putting his name on things.

questions.question: Is this article meant to be the start of an ongoing public debate over these accusations?
questions.audience: New abolitionist trying to understand partnership boundaries
questions.answer: No, the opposite. Silberman frames it as the last time Free the States plans to address these slanderous charges for the foreseeable future. The point is to put a definitive answer on the record so the staff can return to the actual work of fighting abortion.
questions.quote: Thus far, Free the States staff members have combatted these accusations using their personal social media pages, but this article will be the last time we address the subject for the foreseeable future. After this, we will be moving past it to the important work there is to be done.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Direct successor statement on why abolitionists must reject the 'pro-life' label, mirroring the terminology section here.

related.title: Why Do Pro-Lifers Despise Abolitionists?
related.reason: Earlier Silberman piece probing the relational dynamics this article's denials are responding to.

related.reason: First-person counterexample to the 'why insist on a different label' question, showing the practical reasoning behind switching identifiers.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Backs up the affirmation that incrementalism is unbiblical and obstructs abolition.

related.reason: Compact framing of the strategic dispute referenced under the Incrementalists As Brothers and Sisters section.

related.title: The Holiness of God Destroys Pro-Life Incrementalism
related.reason: Theological case parallel to the gospel-centered, anti-idolatry framing Silberman uses to defend the movement.

related.title: Supreme Court Idolatry at Texas Alliance for Life
related.reason: Earlier Silberman essay locating the real idolatry inside the Pro-Life Movement, complementing this article's denial that abolitionism is idolatrous.
